title: Anki Daily Flashcards Calculator 2026 | Hacé Cuentas
description: Calculate daily Anki cards based on vocabulary goals and study time. Free spaced repetition learning calculator.
intro: The Anki Daily Flashcards Calculator tells you exactly how many new cards to add each day—and whether your study time can support that pace—based on your vocabulary target and daily available minutes. It uses Anki's spaced-repetition algorithm (SM-2), where each new card generates roughly 6–10 review repetitions over its first 30 days. The core formula is: New Cards/Day = Target Words ÷ Days Until Deadline, then validated against your available minutes using the empirical benchmark of ~10 minutes per 20 new cards + ~1 minute per 10 due reviews. Overloading new cards is the #1 reason learners abandon Anki decks—this calculator prevents that by aligning your ambition with your actual schedule.
keyTakeaway: **New Cards/Day = Target Vocabulary ÷ Days to Deadline** — e.g., 500 words in 60 days = ~9 new cards/day, requiring ~25–30 min/day once the review backlog matures at day 14.
useCases: A college student preparing for the GRE in 8 weeks who needs to memorize 900 high-frequency words and wants to know if 45 min/day is enough.
A Spanish learner aiming for B2 proficiency (2,000–3,000 vocabulary words) over 6 months who needs a sustainable daily card addition rate.
A medical student building a pharmacology deck of 1,200 drug names, mechanisms, and side effects before a board exam in 10 weeks.
A software engineer studying for the JLPT N3 Japanese exam who needs to absorb 2,000 kanji and vocabulary cards within 4 months.
A high school teacher creating a shared Anki deck for SAT vocabulary prep and wanting to recommend a daily study schedule to students with 20-minute study windows.
explanation:
## How It's Calculated

Anki's SM-2 spaced-repetition algorithm schedules each card for review at exponentially increasing intervals (1 day → 4 days → 10 days → 25 days…). The key implication: every new card you add today creates a *cascade* of future reviews. Empirical data from Anki's open-source community and studies on spaced repetition establish the following benchmarks used in this calculator:

```
// Step 1: Raw new cards per day
New_Cards_Per_Day = Target_Vocabulary_Words / Days_Until_Deadline

// Step 2: Estimate mature review load at steady state (~day 21+)
Daily_Reviews = New_Cards_Per_Day × 7   // ~7 reviews/card in first 30 days (avg interval multiplier)

// Step 3: Estimate total daily time required
Time_For_New_Cards  = New_Cards_Per_Day × 0.5 min   // ~30 sec avg per new card
Time_For_Reviews    = Daily_Reviews × 0.083 min      // ~5 sec avg per review card
Total_Time_Required = Time_For_New_Cards + Time_For_Reviews  // in minutes

// Step 4: Feasibility check
If Total_Time_Required ≤ Available_Daily_Minutes → SUSTAINABLE
If Total_Time_Required >  Available_Daily_Minutes → REDUCE new cards/day or extend deadline
```

The **0.5 min/new card** and **0.083 min/review card** constants are derived from large-scale Anki user data analyzed in Superlearner research and the AnkiStats community (average across language, medical, and academic decks).

---

## Reference Table

| Daily New Cards | Mature Reviews/Day (Day 21+) | Min. Time Needed/Day | Words After 30 Days | Words After 90 Days |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| 5 | ~35 | ~10 min | 150 | 450 |
| 10 | ~70 | ~17 min | 300 | 900 |
| 15 | ~105 | ~24 min | 450 | 1,350 |
| 20 | ~140 | ~32 min | 600 | 1,800 |
| 30 | ~210 | ~47 min | 900 | 2,700 |
| 50 | ~350 | ~78 min | 1,500 | 4,500 |

> ⚠️ Adding 50+ new cards/day is generally unsustainable for most learners—review debt compounds quickly and leads to deck abandonment within 2–3 weeks.

---

## Typical Cases

**Case 1 — GRE Prep (900 words, 60 days, 45 min/day)**
New cards/day = 900 ÷ 60 = **15 cards/day**. Mature review load by week 3: ~105 reviews/day ≈ 24 min. Total time needed ≈ 24 min, well within the 45-minute budget. The learner has a comfortable 21-minute buffer for leeches, hard cards, and image occlusion.

**Case 2 — Medical Boards (1,200 cards, 70 days, 30 min/day)**
New cards/day = 1,200 ÷ 70 ≈ **17 cards/day**. Mature reviews ≈ 119/day → ~27 min total. This is just barely feasible at 30 min/day with zero buffer. Recommended fix: extend the deadline to 85 days (≈14 cards/day, ~22 min) or cut to 1,000 high-yield cards.

**Case 3 — JLPT N3 Vocabulary (2,000 words, 120 days, 60 min/day)**
New cards/day = 2,000 ÷ 120 ≈ **17 cards/day**. Mature reviews ≈ 117/day → ~27 min total. The 60-minute budget is very comfortable; the learner can safely add grammar and kanji reading cards without breaking their schedule.

---

## Common Mistakes

1. **Ignoring the review snowball** — Beginners add 30–50 new cards/day in week 1 and are shocked when they face 300+ reviews/day by week 3. The review count multiplies by ~7× the daily new card count at steady state.
2. **Counting "learning" cards as reviewed** — Anki's default settings show new cards in "learning" steps (e.g., 1 min → 10 min) multiple times per session. Each new card may appear 2–4 times on day 1 alone, inflating perceived session length.
3. **Setting an unrealistic ease floor** — Leaving the default "Ease Factor" at 250% without enabling the free "Load Balancer" or "FSRS" (Free Spaced Repetition Scheduler, available in Anki 23.10+) leads to ease collapse on hard cards, dramatically increasing review times.
4. **Not accounting for weekends or travel** — Missing even 2 consecutive days creates a catch-up pile equal to 3–4× your daily review count. Build a 10–15% time buffer into any schedule using this calculator.
5. **Using one card per "concept" instead of atomic cards** — Cards that ask "Explain oxidative phosphorylation" take 45–90 sec each, blowing up your time estimates. Best practice: one fact per card, target ≤10 sec per review.

---

faq.q: How many new Anki cards per day is realistic for most learners?
faq.a: Research on spaced-repetition habits and Anki user data consistently show that **10–20 new cards/day** is the sustainable sweet spot for most adult learners. At 20 cards/day, you'll accumulate ~140 reviews/day by week 3, requiring roughly 30–35 minutes—manageable for most schedules. Going beyond 30 new cards/day sharply increases dropout risk due to review debt compounding faster than it can be cleared.

faq.q: What is Anki's SM-2 algorithm and how does it affect daily workload?
faq.a: SM-2 (SuperMemo 2) is the spaced-repetition algorithm Anki uses by default, developed by Piotr Woźniak in 1987. It schedules each card at increasing intervals: typically 1 day, 4 days, 10 days, then multiplied by an 'ease factor' (default 250%). Each new card you add today will be reviewed approximately 6–10 times in the first 30 days. Anki 23.10+ also offers FSRS (Free Spaced Repetition Scheduler), a modern ML-based algorithm shown to reduce total reviews by 15–25% at the same retention rate.

faq.q: How long does it take to learn 1,000 vocabulary words with Anki?
faq.a: At 15 new cards/day, you can introduce 1,000 words in about **67 days (~9.5 weeks)**. However, 'learning' means achieving ≥90% retention, which requires those cards to mature—typically 21–30 days after introduction. Budget at least 10–13 weeks total. Total time investment: ~24 min/day × 90 days ≈ **36 hours**. This aligns with foreign language acquisition research showing ~100 hours to reach A2 vocabulary depth.

faq.q: What is a 'mature' card in Anki and why does it matter for time planning?
faq.a: In Anki, a card is considered **'mature' once its interval exceeds 21 days**. Before maturity, cards are reviewed more frequently and consume more of your daily session. The critical planning insight: your review load peaks around days 14–21 when early cards start maturing while new ones keep accumulating. After day 30, if you hold your new card rate steady, total daily time stabilizes—this calculator uses that stabilized estimate for its time-needed output.

faq.q: Is 5 minutes a day with Anki actually effective?
faq.a: Yes, but only for maintenance of an existing deck—not for rapid acquisition. At 5 min/day, you can sustainably add **4–5 new cards/day**, yielding ~150 new words/month. Studies on distributed practice (Cepeda et al., 2006, published in *Psychological Bulletin*) confirm that even brief, consistent sessions dramatically outperform massed study. For active learning goals, 15–30 min/day is the recommended minimum.

faq.q: What happens if I miss a day of Anki reviews?
faq.a: Missing one day means all cards due that day stack on top of the next day's queue—Anki does not redistribute them automatically. At 20 new cards/day, missing **2 days** creates a catch-up burden of ~280–420 extra reviews (roughly 50–70 extra minutes). The practical fix: use Anki's **'Load Balancer' add-on** or simply set your daily new card limit 10–15% below your maximum capacity to absorb occasional missed days without crisis.

faq.q: How does FSRS compare to SM-2 for time efficiency?
faq.a: FSRS (Free Spaced Repetition Scheduler), introduced in Anki 23.10, uses a machine-learning model trained on 1.7 billion Anki reviews. Benchmarks published by its developer (Expertium/open-anki-fsrs) show FSRS achieves the same **90% retention rate with ~20% fewer reviews** compared to SM-2 at default settings. For a learner doing 140 reviews/day under SM-2, FSRS could reduce that to ~112 reviews/day—saving 4–5 minutes daily, or roughly 25 hours per year.

faq.q: How many vocabulary words do I need for language fluency?
faq.a: According to linguist Paul Nation's research and data cited by the **Common European Framework of Reference (CEFR)**: A2 requires ~1,000 word families; B1 ~2,000; B2 ~4,000; C1 ~8,000. The USDA's FSA and NIH's NLM both use readability tools citing ~3,000 high-frequency English words cover 95% of everyday text. For the GRE, ETS officially tests ~500–900 high-frequency academic words. This calculator helps you map whichever target applies to a daily Anki practice plan.
